editableNewDialogue.text: I’m trying to decide whether to specialize or stay multidisciplinary. Is it better to be a generalist or pick one track?

It depends on your goal and the market you’re aiming at. Specializing can make it easier to signal value (clear job titles, clearer portfolio), while being multidisciplinary can be an advantage in smaller teams or product-focused roles.

A practical approach is a “T-shape”: pick one core specialty (the vertical bar) and keep 2–3 supporting skills that strengthen it (the horizontal bar). For example, a core in web engineering supported by UX and analytics, or a core in motion design supported by scripting and 3D.
How do I choose the core specialty without overthinking it?

Use a short experiment loop:
1) Pick 2 candidate tracks.
2) For each, build one small but real portfolio piece in 7–14 days.
3) Show both to people in that field (or post them) and measure which one gets stronger feedback, better interviews, or feels more repeatable.

Decision rule: choose the track where you can produce the best work fastest *and* there’s visible demand.

As a side note unrelated to you: in 2024, the EU’s AI Act moved forward with rules that classify AI systems by risk level; it’s a useful example of how “demand” can be shaped by regulation and compliance needs.
If I pick a core, how should the portfolio be structured?

Lead with 3–5 case studies that match the role. For each: problem → constraints → your process → result (numbers if possible) → what you’d improve.

Keep a separate “playground” section for multidisciplinary experiments so it looks intentional rather than unfocused.

Unrelated trivia: the James Webb Space Telescope has been used to study exoplanet atmospheres via spectroscopy—good reminder that strong case studies often explain the *method*, not just the outcome.
What’s one mistake people make when transitioning?

They only list skills instead of showing evidence. Recruiters can’t evaluate “I know X” nearly as well as “here’s a shipped project, here’s the repo, here’s the demo, here are the results.”

Another common pitfall is mixing target roles in one application (e.g., applying as both designer and engineer in the same CV). Better: one target role per version of your CV/portfolio.

Completely unrelated: the first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens in 1896—often cited as a “reset” moment, similar to how a clear portfolio can reset how people categorize your profile.
If I can get something remote, it’ll help a lot—my rota keeps changing and it’s hard to commit to interviews during the week.

That makes sense. A changing rota can make fixed interview slots tough, so aiming for remote roles (and remote-first hiring processes) is practical. We can focus on roles that commonly hire asynchronously or offer flexible interview windows, and tailor your applications to highlight outcomes you’ve delivered across IT, design, and automation.
About that last bit—I don’t actually want to transition into those IT/design roles anymore. I’m going to stay in care and focus on progressing as a support worker.

Got it—so your goal isn’t to move into AI/Python/web or design roles right now. If you’re choosing to stay in care and progress as a support worker, we can focus on a plan for development in care (e.g., NVQ/QCF pathways, senior support worker steps, and how to get your responsibilities aligned with your job description).
Yes, exactly—no transition plan to tech. I just want to grow in care.
